title: Ulysses
author: Joyce, James
summary: On a mild morning at the Martello tower Buck Mulligan theatrically shaves, mocks and banters with Stephen Dedalus. Mulligan’s loud irreverence, classical quips and insults—calling Stephen Kinch and accusing him of refusing his dying mother—contrast with Stephen’s brooding, memory of his dead mother’s decay and private anguish. They discuss the uneasy lodger Haines, college friends and social scenes, and Mulligan’s schemes and mockery of Irish life. Sea and classical images recur: Greek phrases, snotgreen sea, Thalatta. Tension between public levity and personal grief frames their strained friendship and hints at artistic, social and moral conflicts and psychological interiority tensions persist.

characters.name: Buck Mulligan
characters.personality: brash, theatrical, irreverent, mocking; witty and extroverted
characters.lifespan: present and active in opening scene; survives within the excerpt

characters.name: Malachi Mulligan (alternative name for Buck Mulligan)
characters.personality: boastful, humorous, playful with classical references
characters.lifespan: same as Buck Mulligan (present in scene)

characters.name: Stephen Dedalus
characters.personality: brooding, introspective, artistic, guilt-ridden
characters.lifespan: present in scene; living but grieving his deceased mother

characters.name: Haines
characters.personality: English, uneasy, raving at night (described as ponderous and Saxon)
characters.lifespan: present as uneasy lodger in the tower within the excerpt

characters.name: Stephen’s mother

characters.lifespan: dead (her death and decay are recounted in Stephen's memory)

characters.name: Mulligan’s aunt
characters.personality: practical, disapproving (refuses close association with Stephen)
characters.lifespan: alive and mentioned as withholding support in the excerpt

characters.name: Ursula

characters.lifespan: alive and referenced as Mulligan’s servant

characters.personality: lighthearted, referenced by Mulligan (calls the sea)
characters.lifespan: mentioned only by name; alive in referenced social circle

unusual_words.context: Appears in the intoned phrase 'Introibo ad altare Dei.'
Part of the Latin liturgical phrase 'Introibo ad altare Dei.'
Final word of 'Introibo ad altare Dei.'

unusual_words.definition: not fastened by a girdle; loose
unusual_words.context: Describes the yellow dressinggown 'ungirdled' behind Mulligan.
Mulligan jokes his name is 'two dactyls.'
Mulligan says his name has a 'Hellenic ring.'
Mulligan calls Haines 'the jejune jesuit.'
Describes Haines as a 'ponderous Saxon.'

unusual_words.definition: used here to mean English or Anglo-Saxon
unusual_words.context: Mulligan refers to Haines as a 'ponderous Saxon.'
Stephen asks 'Where is his guncase?' about Haines.
Buck Mulligan cries 'Scutter!' while searching pockets.
Mulligan borrows Stephen’s 'noserag' to wipe his razor.
Mulligan coins 'snotgreen' as a new art colour for poets.

unusual_words.definition: coined compound evoking a physical tightening sensation
unusual_words.context: Mulligan describes the sea as the 'scrotumtightening sea.'
Begins the Greek phrase 'Epi oinopa ponton.'
Part of 'Epi oinopa ponton' referring to the sea.
Appears in the phrase 'Epi oinopa ponton.'
Mulligan cries 'Thalatta! Thalatta!' meaning 'the sea'.

unusual_words.definition: of extreme northern regions; here used figuratively
unusual_words.context: Mulligan says 'I’m hyperborean as much as you.'
Mulligan calls Stephen a 'lovely mummer.'

unusual_words.definition: contemptuous adjective meaning worthless or diseased
unusual_words.context: Mulligan says 'God knows what poxy bowsy left them off.'

unusual_words.definition: slang for a drunk or messy person
unusual_words.context: Used in 'poxy bowsy' describing who left off the trousers.

unusual_words.definition: coinage/variant humorously referring to 'secondhand leg' or trousers
unusual_words.context: Mulligan jokes 'Secondleg they should be.'
Mulligan says 'You’ll look spiffing in them.'

unusual_words.definition: abbreviated slang for 'general paralysis of the insane'
unusual_words.context: Mulligan alleges Stephen has 'g. p. i.'
Mulligan says someone made tin 'by selling jalap to Zulus.'
Mulligan mentions 'The rage of Caliban' metaphorically.

unusual_words.definition: reference to Saint John Chrysostom or golden-mouthed speaker
unusual_words.context: Mulligan whistles and says 'Chrysostomos.'

unusual_words.definition: a high-ranking member of the clergy
unusual_words.context: Mulligan’s shadowed face recalled 'a prelate.'
Stephen notes Mulligan’s face was 'equine in its length.'

unusual_words.definition: member of the Society of Jesus; used as an epithet
unusual_words.context: Mulligan insults Stephen as 'you fearful jesuit!'
Mulligan jokes about 'those white corpuscles.'

unusual_words.definition: archaic variant of 'woeful' meaning very sad or pitiable
unusual_words.context: Mulligan calls Haines 'A woful lunatic!'
Mulligan threatens 'we’ll give him a ragging.'
A student exclaims 'I don’t want to be debagged!'
A hand is 'plucking the harpstrings' in a poetic image.

unusual_words.definition: mythic or literary name used in a song
unusual_words.context: Lines quoted include 'For Fergus rules the brazen cars.'
Stephen remembers his mother 'within its loose brown graveclothes.'
Describes the 'odour of wax and rosewood' from the corpse.

unusual_words.definition: a fragrant hardwood; used descriptively of smell
unusual_words.context: The corpse gives off an odour of 'wax and rosewood.'

unusual_words.definition: digestive fluid; used vividly to describe illness
unusual_words.context: A bowl held 'the green sluggish bile' from his mother.
He sees bodies 'cut up into tripes in the dissectingroom.'
Stephen mentions 'cut up into tripes in the dissectingroom.'

unusual_words.definition: pejorative for a drudge or lowly person
unusual_words.context: Mulligan calls Stephen 'Ah, poor dogsbody!'
Mulligan 'pinched it out of the skivvy’s room.'
Stephen calls the mirror 'the cracked lookingglass of a servant.'
Mulligan says 'Tell that to the oxy chap downstairs.'

unusual_words.definition: historic British coin (slang for a fee)
unusual_words.context: Mulligan says 'touch him for a guinea.'
Mulligan says 'His old fellow made his tin by selling jalap.'

unusual_words.definition: South African people (used to indicate exotic market)
unusual_words.context: Used in 'selling jalap to Zulus' to mock commercial schemes.
Mulligan mentions 'some bloody swindle or other.'

unusual_words.definition: slang denoting pale-faced students or Englishmen
unusual_words.context: Crowd described as 'Palefaces: they hold their ribs with laughter.'
A deaf gardener 'pushes his mower on the sombre lawn.'
The deaf gardener is described as 'aproned.'
A scared calf’s face 'gilded with marmalade.'

unusual_words.definition: navel or central point (Greek origin), used poetically
unusual_words.context: Phrase 'To ourselves... new paganism... omphalos.'

unusual_words.definition: archaic for blades or stalks of grass
unusual_words.context: The mower watches 'the dancing motes of grasshalms.'

unusual_words.definition: religious practices outside the dominant faith; here rhetorical
unusual_words.context: Mulligan utters 'new paganism...' in a flippant aside.

unusual_words.definition: reference to Ignatius Loyola (founder of Jesuits)
unusual_words.context: Mulligan says 'Chuck Loyola, Kinch, and come on down.'

unusual_words.definition: Gaelic term for an English person
unusual_words.context: Mulligan says 'The Sassenach wants his morning rashers.'

unusual_words.definition: lacking sequence or consistency; here meaning inconsistent
unusual_words.context: Mulligan claims 'I’m inconsequent.'
Mulligan comes from the 'stairhead' and booms down the stairhead.

unusual_words.definition: support or rest for a gun; here a round stone parapet
unusual_words.context: They sit and stand upon the 'round gunrest.'

unusual_words.definition: low protective wall along the edge of a roof or terrace
unusual_words.context: Mulligan props his mirror on the 'parapet.'
Used to describe Mulligan’s 'smokeblue mobile eyes.'

unusual_words.definition: surgical instrument or metaphor for sharp instrument
unusual_words.context: Stephen fears 'the lancet of my art' as Mulligan fears his.

unusual_words.definition: to make Greek or adopt Greek culture
unusual_words.context: Mulligan fantasizes 'if you and I could ... Hellenise it.'
Mulligan says 'Isn’t the sea what Algy calls it.'

unusual_words.definition: made of brass; bold or impudent
unusual_words.context: Quoted line 'For Fergus rules the brazen cars.'
The 'twining stresses, two by two' describes poetic imagery.

unusual_words.definition: wearing an apron (duplicate entry appears in text)
unusual_words.context: Describes the gardener 'aproned' while mowing the lawn.
Mulligan wears a 'yellow dressinggown.'
Mulligan asks if he can get the aunt to 'fork out twenty quid.'

unusual_words.definition: a state of fear or panic
unusual_words.context: Mulligan asks 'Were you in a funk?' after Haines’s raving.
Stephen watches the 'mailboat clearing the harbourmouth of Kingstown.'
The mailboat clears the 'harbourmouth of Kingstown.'
Mulligan’s 'strong wellknit trunk' is described when he laughs.

unusual_words.definition: narrow stripe in fabric resembling hairline pattern
unusual_words.context: Mulligan mentions 'a lovely pair with a hair stripe, grey.'
Mulligan notes 'I don’t whinge like some hired mute from Lalouette’s.'
Woodshadows float 'from the stairhead seaward where he gazed.'

unusual_words.definition: to make bubbling sounds as liquid does
unusual_words.context: Mulligan 'gurgling in his throat' while making the sign of the cross.

unusual_words.definition: the lower part of a person’s cheek or jaw
unusual_words.context: Mulligan’s 'sullen oval jowl' is used to describe his face.
